The other exports of C. are honey and wax, to Venice, Trieste, and Messina; glass to Italy; and also some sugar, cordage, cloth, &c .- The principal imports of Croatia, for its own consumption, consist in sea-salt, Dalmatian wine, oil, sugar, maize, and hemp. The salt is derived chiefly from the Venetian islands, and also from Barletta and Manfredonia in the kingdom of Naples. The consumption of this article in the military districts alone was valued in 1801 at 230,000 florins. The annual importation of wine from Dalmatia amounts to 700,000 florins, and of brown sugar, for the refining houses at Fiume, to about 600,000 florins. A considerable quantity of maize is brought from Romania and the Venetian islands, which is used by the inhabitants of the coast, and of the adjacent mountains of Carniola, in making their bread and polenta: in the E districts of C., however, as well as in Slavonia and Hungary, this grain is used only for feeding pigs. The commerce of this country suffers greatly from the want of proper means of conveyance. None of its navigable rivers run towards the Adriatic, but, taking an easterly direction, fall into the Danube, whose lengthened course, after it receives their waters, renders them of very little service in expediting the conveyance of its produce towards the coast. These rivers are also of very difficult navigation. Immense trees, half-sunk in their bed, often require the greatest address on the part of the sailors to avoid; and indeed, the circumstance of the rivers being so frequently liable to change their course, precludes all expectation that these defects will ever be completely remedied. Attempts were made some years ago to facilitate the navigation of the Save. At Agram it is a fine river, with water enough to float barges of 200 tons. A great quantity of corn and brandy is annually conveyed up this river from the Banat. It was also in contemplation, in 1771, to render the Culpa navigable from Carlstadt to Brod, which would have been of vast importance to the commerce of Hungary. The project was renewed in 1800 by a society at the head of which was the bishop of Agram; but no decided measures have yet been taken for its accomplishment. But even in its present state, this river is open for large boats in spring and autumn; and a steamer descends the Save weekly from Sizzek at the confluence of the Kulpa, to Semlin opposite Belgrade. The only method of transporting merchandise from Carlstadt to the coast is by land, which is so troublesome and expensive, that the merchants of Fiume and other seaports can draw their supplies at a cheaper rate from the Italian and Venetian states. The principal roads in this direction are the Caroline way and the Josephine way. The former, which reaches from Carlstadt to Fiume, and is about 85 m. in length, was formed in 1726 by Charles VI. It is cut entirely in the rock of the mountains which it traverses, but is very injudiciously planned, and almost totally unfit for waggons. Another road has been executed in the same direction, with more gentle declivities, and in every way more convenient for passengers: nothing indeed can be more beautifully constructed than this road, known as the Ludovica road, and it is maintained in excellent order. The Josephine way lies between Carlstadt and Zengg, and passes over the Kapella. It is both shorter and less mountainous than the Caroline way. The other roads are very indifferent. The route from Carlstadt to Austria passes through Carniola and Carinthia, by Motting, Laybach, and Klagenfurth; that from Agram is by Cilli in Styria, and CROATIA. Volkermarkt in Carinthia; and that from Warasdin is also through Styria and Carinthia, by Pettau and Mahrburg. The road to Hungary takes the direction of Kopreinitz and Dernje, and that to Slavonia is by Ludbring. Government.] Until the recent political re-arrangements of the different territories composing the Austrian empire, C. and Slavonia had the same laws and constitution as the rest of Hungary, except in one or two particulars, in which they enjoyed special privileges. The counties sent deputies to the diet, as other parts of Hungary; and the county meetings were held in the same way; but in addition to this, they sometimes held what was called Comitia regnorum Croatiæ et Slavoniæ, or 'Diets of the kingdoms of C. and Slavonia;' and they occasionally exercised the right of refusing to obey or to adopt the acts of the General diet. Thus when the General diet repealed the law excluding all Protestants from holding real property within the country, the C. refused to sanction or adopt it. In fact neither Lutheran nor Calvinist is permitted to make public profession of his belief in C. while religious toleration in Hungary is unlimited. The ban or executive magistracy of C. stood also completely separate from the control of the palatine of Hungary. C. and Slavonia are to be entirely separated from Hungary, but the position of Dalmatia is reserved for further deliberation. The ancient dignity of ban of the kingdoms of C. and Slavonia is to be revived; the ban remains as statthalter, subject to the orders of the ministry of the empire. A minister is to be appointed to watch the interests of the two provinces, responsible to the reichstag only for those acts of the government which bear his signature. The direction of the military on the military frontier, which the C. landtag proposed should be confided to the C. civil authorities, is reserved to the emperor and his generals. By the constitution the emperor is commander-in-chief of all the troops of the empire. The command-in-chief in the prov. will remain, therefore, in the hands of the ban. The national Slavonic is to remain the official language; but consideration is had for the communication with the authorities in other provs., where a different language is spoken. The Crown is to retain the sole right of appointing to all offices. The registration of landed property and the regulation of the feudal servitude question are reserved for future consideration, together with the regulation of the price of and trade in salt. The C. and Slavonian landtag, which was assembled in 1848, is declared to be dissolved. The patent itself refers in terms of the highest praise to the services rendered in 1849 by the Cs. and Slavonians. The Grundgesetz has also been published for the C.- Slavonian and Banatian-Servian military frontier. It is less important than the above, as it refers more completely to the peculiar institutions of this part of the empire. The old military organization which has existed since the invasion by the Turks, and their repulse by Sobieski, is retained in all its parts, and remains subordinate to the supreme military authorities of the empire. The inhabitants are subject to military laws for military offences, but for all others to the general laws of the empire; they are bound to serve at home and abroad when called upon; the tenure of land carries with it the duty of serving as a soldier.—We may here observe, that when C. attained its present extent in the reign of Leopold I. it was divided by that prince into counties, to which he nominated governors, called obergespanne, who, however, did not then enter upon their office, as the counties were immediately submitted to other administrators, named ban, a word signifying 'lord' in the C. language. They were, however, effectually installed into their office in the reign of Maria Theresa, and made responsible to a council, whose president was the ban of C. The present political division of C. is into the civil, provincial, or constitutional department, and the military department. In the former are comprehended the maritime districts known under the name of 'the Littoral,' and the three counties of Agram, Warasdin, and Kreutz. The military department intervenes between the NW corner of Turkey and the Adriatic, and is governed by a military administration, which was developed as an offensive and defensive system in the long wars with the Porte in the 16th, 17th, and 18th cents. The king of Hungary is regarded as the only landlord in this district, and the peasantry pay no rent, and scarcely any taxes, but in lieu of both every house with 3 sons furnishes a soldier, with 5 sons 2 soldiers, and so on, who serve from 20 to 23 years, receiving only their uniform and arms from government. The district, which is divided geographically into regiments in place of counties, comprehends, 1. The military district of Carlstadt, which has 4 regiments, viz.: the district of the regiment of Licca, of Ottochacz, of Ogulin, and of Szluin. 2. The military boundary of the frontiers, called the Banal-granze, which is divided into 2 regiments, viz.: the district of the first regiment of the frontiers, and that of the second. 3. The generalat or military division of Warasdin, composed also of 2 regiments, viz.: the district of the regiment of Kreutz, and that of St. George. The whole system of military organization furnishes 60,000 men, at all times available for active service, and capable of being called into the field in a few hours by signals made from hill to hill. Organization of the Granz Comitats.] For the following minute and most interesting account of the unique organization of the Austrian military frontiers, we are exclusively indebted to the Rev. R. Gleig's volumes on Bohemia and Hungary. [London, 3 vols. 8vo., 1839.] From New Orsova, a good way on the other side of the Danube, to the shores of the Adriatic and the Dalmatian frontier, there extends a belt of territory for the most part very narrow, with here and there a patch thrown down, as it were, in the midst of districts differently constituted, and one remarkable tongue of land interposing between Croatia and Lower Hungary. Within this belt military law, or rather military jurisdiction, entirely prevails. There every man capable of bearing arms is a soldier; every officer is likewise a magistrate; and from year's end to year's end, neither officer nor soldier can be said to live otherwise than he would were an organized enemy in his front. The whole of this district, which—except in the tongue of land alluded to above—never exceeds, where it is the broadest, 30 m. in width, is divided into 14 counties, each of which constitutes within itself a compact community; though all are held together by the bond of a general responsibility to the Crown and its representatives. The supreme authorities which represent the Crown in this district, consist first of a governor, or commanderin-chief, whose head-quarters are established at Peterwardein; and next of one or more generals subordinate to him. The fortified places that lie within the limits of these counties, or places which take rank as festungs or forts, such as Semlin, Brod, Alt Gradisca, and many more, being occupied by garrisons from the regular Austrian army, have each its distinct commandant, and each its own peculiar jurisdiction. The land, whether cultivated or waste, along the whole of this border, belongs exclusively to the supreme government. The inhabitants of a frontier county are all, more or less, agriculturists; that is to say, every family obtains a holding under the Crown, and makes the most of it. Each household, moreover, grows its own wool, weaves its own cloth, and makes its own wearing apparel; while for the holding which produces the materials of all these, rent is paid in labour. The men, besides the military service which is exacted from them, cultivate, in addition to their own farms, both the officers' portions and those which the government keeps in its own hands. From the latter toil they may indeed purchase an exemption, provided they contrive by any means to obtain the command of money; but as this seldom, if ever, happens to be the case, the privilege is rather nominal than real. Moreover, there are public works, such as the making of roads, building of forts and magazines, and the construction and repair of the officers' lodgings, in which, when required, they must busy themselves; the time which they spend in these being carried to the credit side of their account with the government. Such is the kind of rent which each man pays for his farm, and which is regularly computed for him by a public accountant. According to the extent and value of his holding, he owes to the government a certain quantity of la- | with any regularity, but generally consist of a num- CROATIA. labour, or else a certain amount of money, for which the produce of that labour is assumed to be an equivalent. It is not, however, by the works of his hands alone, during a given number of days in the year, that the borderer purchases his right to till his own fields, and gather in their produce. The law imposes upon every male, so long as he is capable of bearing arms, the liability to serve as a soldier. Even in times of peace, each county is required to keep on foot 2 battalions of 1,200 men a-piece; while in times of war, the levy is increased to 4 battalions. But this is not all: should an exigency arise, the emperor has the right of ordering out the entire male population. In this case, all between the ages of 18 and 36 are absolutely at the disposal of the State; all above 18 and below it, from the age of 12, must arm to defend their own firesides. Hence, if the active battalions, as they are called, were marched away en masse into Italy or elsewhere, the frontier duty would still be carried on by old men and boys. Partly to protect their frontier against outrages, partly as a sanitory cordon, and partly that the Austrian army may be increased, at a very moderate expense, by 30,000 superb infantry, the government requires that these shall be continually embodied in each county, one regiment, consisting of 2 battalions, that is, of 2,500 men. Chosen by ballot, out of the portion of the males whose ages neither fall short, nor exceed the specified limits, these battalions are ready at any moment, to take the field, though they never, unless an emergency occur, assemble to manœuvre, or exercise as regiments, except during a single month in every year. Till the harvest is gathered in, on the contrary, the men dwell in their own homes, and in the bosom of their own families, pursuing their daily occupations like other peasants, and putting on the military dress only one day in each week, every man as his turn comes round, to take, what well deserves to be termed, the out post duty. For along the whole Turkish frontier, there is a chain of posts established,-picquets in the most absolute sense of the term,-which keeping, from their elevated station-houses, a sharp look-out during the day, push forward their sentries at night, and so place them, that each shall take his station within easy hail of those on the right and left of him. The consequence is, that no commercial intercourse whatever takes place between the two countries, but at points that are set apart for the purpose; and that individual adventurers never pass to and fro, except by eluding the notice of the guards, or forcing the sentries. The countries thus organized are governed, both in their civil and military concerns, by colonels, majors, captains, lieutenants, sergeant-majors, sergeants, and corporals. Over the entire county the colonel reigns, or appears to reign, supreme. He keeps a map of his territory, in which every house, field, and even tree, is set forth; he visits the several dwellings of his people once, at least, in every year; and he receives reports from the functionaries that are under him, as well periodically, as when some sudden exigency may call for them. Next in influence, as in rank, to the colonel, are the lieutenant-colonels and majors, who superintend respectively their half-battalions, each of 6 companies, -visiting household by household once in every half-year, and receiving the reports of the captains, that they may be forwarded to head-quarters. Last of all come the captains, whose vigilance is in like manner taxed by the necessity which is imposed upon them, of executing monthly inspections, and who employ both their lieutenants and sub-officers in the superintendence of their respective squads, till the order of responsibility is completed. With all this show of power, and much of its substance to boot, it is not to be imagined that either colonels, majors, or captains, are absolute in their respective departments. What the colonel is to the whole county, the captain, no doubt, is to his portion of it; yet both colonel and captain are checked in all directions by a machinery admirably adapted to the uses which it is meant to serve. There is associated, for example, with each captain, an officer of finance, or economy, on whom the superintendence of all the fiscal affairs of the company devolve. There is a well-organized machinery in every county, and in every department or district of each county, for the administration of civil and criminal justice. Once a-week, at the chief station of the company, or circle, a sort of sessions is held by the officers of finance, where meet together the sergeantmajor, 2 sergeants, and 2 corporals, all, like the officer, selected from the finance department. In addition to these, the colonel nominates two heads of families—men of known good character and respectability; and, lo, the court is complete! From the decision of this district court, however, an appeal lies to a court similarly constituted, at head-quarters, where, over and above the finance officers, 3 gentlemen, learned in the law, deliver their opinions. The same tribunal which decides in civil cases, tries parties that are charged with punishable offences. Over the troops actually enrolled, indeed, the authority of the officers is supreme; they carry on the discipline of their respective battalions exactly as military discipline is preserved elsewhere. But persons not attached to these battalions, whether they be men or women, are brought before the monthly sessions, if suspected of crime, and dealt with according to the decision of the judges. The punishments, again, are flogging with a stick, imprisonment, working in chains, and even death. It is worthy of remark, however, that grave charges are not dealt with except at the headquarters of the county, where a supreme court of criminal justice is presumed to be always sitting. It consists of a chef-de-battailon, as president, an auditor, who acts likewise as judge-advocate, 2 captains, 2 sergeant-majors, 2 corporals, and 2 privates. Cities and towns.] None of the villages are built ber of huts scattered up and down at a considerable distance from each other, with their kitchen-gardens and yards all fenced round with high palings to protect the stock from wolves in winter. The houses are for the most part constructed of wood, except upon the coast, and in some of the principal cities and towns, where stones or brick are used, and sometimes marble. The principal cities are: Agram or Zagrab on the Save, the capital and a bishop's see, which is a well-built and populous town, containing 10,000 inhabitants; Carlstadt on the Culpa, built in 1579, and defended by a fortress; Warasdin, situated in a plain on the Drave, with a castle, and 8,000 inhabitants; Fiume, a sea-port on the Adriatic, with a citadel and a tolerable harbour; Zengg, a well-fortified little town near the sea, containing several churches and convents, and 2,744 inhabitants; and Carlsbago, a trading town S of Zengg, with a good harbour, and 995 inhabitants.—Demian Tubleau Geographique et Politique des Royaumes de Hongrie, &c. History.] In the earliest period of history C. was occupied by the Pannonii: and on the subjugation of that people by Augustus, it became a province of Illyria. The Goths overrun it in 489; and in 640 the Chrobates, a Wendish tribe from Bohemia, took possession of it, and founded various small zupanies or duchies in it. Dircislav L, the son of Crescimer, an archizupan or archduke of the 10th cent. assumed the title of king of Croatia. His dominions appear to have extended over Dalmatia and Bosnia. In 1100, C. was incorporated with Hungary. With Slavonia, Dalmatia, and some parts of Hungary, under the title of the States of Illyria, it was, for a time, governed by a special board at Vienna.

CROBO HILLS, a group of isolated hills of Up per Guinea, in the kingdom of Ashantee, to the NNE of Ningo river, about 12 m. from the shore. They are of singular configuration, and rise to the height of 950 ft. above sea-level.
CROC HARBOUR, an inlet of the NE coast of Newfoundland, 5 m. SSW of Hare bay, in N lat. 51° 10'. W long. 55° 40'.

CROCE (SANTA), a town of Sicily, in the prov. and 45 m. SW of Syracuse, on a small river which flows into the Mediterranean, 2 m. NNW of Cape Scalambra. Pop. 2,093. In the vicinity are the ruins of Caucana. - Also a cape 15 m. N of Syracuse, 3 m. NE of Augusta, in N lat. 37° 15', E long. 15° 15'.—Also a town of Austria, in the circle of Trieste, circle and 13 m. ESE of Gorizia, on the r. bank of the Wippach. Pop. 1,037. It has an ancient fortress.—Also a village in the circle and 8 m. NW of Trieste, on the coast-road to Gorizia. In the vicinity are quarries of fine grey marble.—Also a commune and town of Tuscany, in the prov. of Florence, 4 m. NNW of San-Mineato, on the r. bank of the Arno. Pop. 5,391. It has silk and woollen manufactures.
CROCE (MONTE DI SANTA), a mountain-range of the island of Cyprus, extending about 60 m. from E to W, and terminating in Cape Salizano or Pifanio.

CROCE-DI-MAGLIANO (SANTA), a town of Naples, in the prov. of Sannio, district and 8 m. SE of Larino, and 23 m. NE of Campobasso, in a fine plain. Pop. 3,781. It is surrounded by walls, and has a handsome church. Fairs are held here twice a-year.
CROCE-DI-MORCONE (SANTA), a town of Naples, in the prov. of Sannio, district and 13 m. SSE of Campobasso. Pop. 3,311. It has 2 churches and a convent, and in the environs is a sulphureous spring.

CROCQ, or Croc, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Creuse, arrond. of Aubusson. The cant. comprises 15 com. Pop. in 1831, 11,816; in 1841, 12,384. The town, which was formerly fortified, is situated on the summit of a mountain, near the I. bank of the Tardes, 12 m. SE of Aubusson. Pop. in 1846, 1,045. It has a considerable trade in horses, cattle, sheep, pigs, common hats, CRO mercery, earthenware, and agricultural implements. Eleven fairs take place annually here.
CROCK-STREET, a hamlet, partly in the parishes of Donyatt and Ilminster, Somerset. Pop. 54.
CROCKENHILL, a hamlet, partly in the parishes of St. Mary Cray and Eynesford, Kent. Pop. 470.
CROCKER-HILL, a hamlet in the p. of Boxgrove, Sussex. Pop. 52.
CROCKERNE-TORR, a high rock in the forest of Dartmoor, p. of Lydford, Devon, where the parliament for stannary causes is held. The table and seats are hewn out of the rock.
CROCKETTSVILLE, a village of Russell co., in the state of Alabama, U. S., 182 m. ESE of Tuscaloosa.
CROCKGLASS, a mountain of co. Donegal, which rises 1,295 ft. in height above sea-level.
CROCODILE ISLANDS, a group off the N coast of Arnheim Land, Australia, in Castlereagh bay, in N lat. 12° 10′, E long. 134° 30′.
CRODALLE, a village of France, in the dep. of the Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Londinieres, 32 m. NNE of Rouen. Pop. 367. It has a glass-work.
CROES AND BERWYN, a township in the p. of Caron-ys-Clawdd, Cardiganshire. Pop. 256.

CROFF, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, regency and 28 m. NE of Treves, circle and 9 m. E of Wittlich, on the l. bank of the Moselle. Pop. 1,123. The environs produce wine, and contain mines of copper.
CROFORD AND NUNNINGTON, tythings in the p. of Wiveliscombe, Somerset. Pop. 445.

CROFT, a parish of Herefordshire, 5 m. NNW of Leominster. Area 1,980 acres. Pop. 144.—Also a p. of Leicestershire, 53 m. ENE of Hinckley, bounded on the E by the Soar. Area 1,010 acres. Pop. 321.—Also a p. of Lincolnshire, 7 m. ENE of Spilsby, on the Steeping. Area 5,840 acres. Pop. 649. -Also a parish and township of the N. R. of Yorkshire, 94 m. NE of Richmond, intersected by the Croft branch of the Darlington railway. Area 7,060 acres. Pop. 744. The township is 31 m. S of Darlington, on the S bank of the Tees. Area 4,700 acres. Pop. 422. See also Southworth and Croft.

CROFTON, a parish of the W. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. SE of Wakefield. Area 970 acres. Pop. 389.— Also a township in the p. of Thursby, Cumberland, 34 m. ENE of Wigton, on the N bank of the Wampool, and on the Carlisle railway. Pop. 80.-Also a chapelry in the p. of Tichfield, Hants. Pop. 809.
CROFTON AND WOLFHALL, a tything in the p. of Great Bedwin, Wilts. Pop. 180.
CROGEN-IDDON AND CROGEN-W-LADIES. townships in the p. of Llangollen, Glyn-Train division, Denbighshire. Pop. respectively 197 and 151.
CROGENNET AND MORFA, a township in the p. of Llangelynin, Merionethshire. Pop. 390.
CROGHAN, a township of Lewis co., in the state of New York, U. S., 141 m. NW of Albany. Its surface is hilly, and its soil in some parts indifferent.

CROGHAN, a parish of King's co., 3 m. N of Philipstown. Area 5,794 acres. Pop. 915.—Also a village of co. Roscommon, on the road from Boyle to Strokestown, consisting of about 20 dwellings.— Also a mountain in co. Mayo, running along the vales of Ballymonelly and Glenco.

CROGHAN, a rivulet, partly in counties Leitrim, Longford, and Cavan. It rises to the N of the Cairn or Clonhugh mountains; forms a beautiful chain of lakes at Castle-Hamilton; and falls into Lough Oughter.
CROGHAN, or SLIEVE-CROGHAN, a mountain of co. Mayo, on the W verge of the island of Achill, 2,500 ft. in height, and rising perpendicularly from the water's edge.
CROGHANE, or CROHANE, a parish of co. Tipperary, 21 m. E of Killenaule, intersected by a headstream of the Lingay river, and by the road from Killenaule to Callan. Area 5,434 acres. Pop. 1,817.

CROGHAN-KINSHELA, a mountain-range on the S verge of co. Wicklow, 4 m. S of Arklow, the summit of which is 2,064 ft. in height above sea-level. It is famous as the site of the "Wicklow gold-mines." It is a little more than half-a-century since gold was first discovered in the streams and valleys leading from the C. mountains for upwards of 10 m., in various directions; and as the deposits of it had been accumulating since the earth assumed its present form, very large quantities of gold were obtained with slight labour,—some pieces worth as much as £80. Thousands of persons from all parts of the country flocked to these valleys of gold, and for a few years some realized large sums of money. The washinggrounds soon became scenes of anarchy and confusion; and government at last sent a regiment of infantry, took possession of the place, and commenced a more regular system of workings upon their own account. The commissioners managed to make the income meet the expenditure, but nothing more; and the rebellion in Ireland put an end to the whole affair. The commissioners, it would appear, made no improvements whatever in the common mode of washing adopted by the peasantry, which was of the rudest description. A company of infantry remained to guard the place for several years; but when they were at last removed, the peasantry continued to search the streams for gold, particularly in the summer-season, and, generally speaking, made on an average very good wages by their uncertain avocation. It is calculated that they produced about £2,000 worth of the purest gold every year. Some years since, a license to renew the C. workings was obtained from the Crown by some gentlemen. They only worked four months, during which time they sold gold to the value of £1,800: a quarrel with the owner of the land put an end to their working. Neither the commissioners appointed by the Crown, nor the lessees of the Crown have made one solitary experiment, nor one trial, to discover the source from whence all this gold has been and continues to be produced. The mode of washing adopted in the C. workings is ruder than any plan adopted by the Africans. The celebrated work of Sir R. Murchison, on the Ural mountains, called attention to the Wicklow district, in consequence of the extraordinary analogy existing between the two places, particularly as regards the immense load or body of magnetic iron ore that carries itself in such vast strength through the Carysfoot and Croghan mountains, as well as those of Ural. The Wicklow gold is of the purest quality, worth £3 18s. 6d. an ounce. [Mining Journal.]
CROGHANNE, a mountain of co. Kerry. It is of conical form, and is separated by a narrow glen from the famous mountain mass of Mangerton.
CROGLIN, a parish of Cumberland, 44 m. NNE of Cumberland, on the N bank of the Croglin. Area 9,180 acres. Pop. 336.

CROIA, or CROYA, a sanjak of Turkey in Europe, in the Albanian pashalik of Scutari. Its greatest length from N to S is about 53 m.; and it is 40 m. in average breadth. It is bounded on the N by the sanjaks of | in the dep. of the Vosges, cant. of Fraize, 7 m. SE Scutari and Jacova; on the E by those of Prisrendi and Okrida; on the S by the latter sanjak; and on the W by those of Tirana and Scutari. It presents a generally mountainous surface, and in the N is the lofty summit of Kerubi. Besides the Drino, by which it is bordered on the E and N, it is watered by the Fande, Oros, Matt, Vardazo, Izmo, and several other minor streams. It is extensively covered with wood, and affords corn in great abundance. The pop. con- CRO sists of Albanians, Servians, and Bulgarians.-The town of Croia or Ak-Hissar, cap. of the preceding sanjak, is situated on a hill, between the Izmo and Vardazo, 8 m. N of Tirana, and 40 m. SE of Scutari. Pop. 6,000. It is defended by a fortress, erected by Scanderbeg.
CROIS-BARS (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Mur-de-Barrez. Pop. 1,771.
CROISETTES (Les), a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, district and 4 m. NW of Lausanne, on the Jorat, at an alt. of 2,421 above sea-level.

CROISIC (LE), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Loire-Inferieure, arrond. of Savenay. The cant. comprises 2 com. Pop. in 1831, 5,931; in 1841, 6,136. The town is pleasantly situated on a small gulf of the Atlantic, which forms an excellent port, 30 m. W of Savenay, and 40 m. WNW of Nantes, at the embouchures of the Vilaine and Loire. Pop. in 1841, 2,539. It has a bank, a hydrographical school, and a custom-house; and possesses an active trade in fish, salt from the adjacent salt-marshes, and soda. A fair is held once a-year. Tin is found in the environs. The fisheries connected with this port were formerly of great extent.

CROISILLE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Vienne, cant. of Châteauneuf, 24 m. SE of Limoges, on the highest summit of the mountains which form the SE side of the basin of the Vienne. Pop. 980.

CROISILLES, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of the Pas-de-Calais, arrond. of Arras. The cant. comprises 27 com. Pop. in 1831, 16,483; in 1841, 11,995. The village is 9 m. SE of Arras. Pop. in 1846, 1,566.
CROISSANVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Calvados, cant. of Mezidon. Pop. 243. It has manufactures of lace, and a cotton spinning-mill.
CROISSET, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Maronne, and com. of Canteleu, on the Seine. Pop. 941.
CROISY-LA-HAYE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Argueil. Pop. 991. It has a cotton spinning-mill.
CROIX, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nord, cant. of Roubaix, 4 m. NE of Lille. Pop. in 1846, 1,574.
CROIX (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Indre-et-Loire, cant. of Blere. Pop. 1,171.
CROIX - AVRANCHIN (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Manche, cant. of Saint- James. Pop. 1,049.
CROIX-CHAPEAU, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Charente-Inférieure, cant. of La Jarrie. Pop. 734.
CROIX-EN-BRIE (LA), a village of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Marne, cant. of Nangis, 11 m. W of Provins. Pop. 700. It has an ancient castle and church.
CROIX (COL-DE-LA), a pass of the Cottian Alps, on the confines of France and Italy, 6 m. N of Viso, and 24 m. SE of Briancon.
CROIX-HELLE'AN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, cant. and 2 m. from Josselin. Pop. 766.
CROIX-AUX-MINES (LA), a commune of France, of Saint-Dié. Pop. 1,562. In the environs is a mine of argentiferous lead.
CROIX-DE-MISSION (LA), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Loire, cant. of Saint Etienne and com. of Montaud. Pop. 1,836.
CROIX-MANE, a village of France, in the dep. of the Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Pavilly, 17 m. NW of Rouen. Pop. 840. It has fairs twice a-year.
CROIX-SUR-MEUSE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. and on the r. bank of the Meuse, cant. of Saint-Mihiel, 18 m. NNW of Commercy. Pop. 1,077.

CROIX-ROUSSE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. and on the r. bank of the Rhone, cant. of Lyons, of which town it forms a suburb. Pop. in 1841, 18,790. It has extensive manufactories of silk, dyeworks, printing-mills, &c., and conducts a considerable trade in wine.
CROIX-SAINT-LENFROY (LA), a town of France, in the dep. and on the r. bank of the Eure, cant. of Gaillon, 8 m. SSE of Louviers. Pop. 730.
CROIX-SAINT-OUEN (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. and near the l. bank of the Ouse, cant. and 4 m. SW of Compiègne. Pop. 1,143.

CROIX (SAINTE), or PASSAMAQUODDY, a river of N America, which rises a little to the S of the parallel of 46° N, in two branches, and flows in a prevailingly SSE course to Passamaquoddy bay, an inlet on the N coast of the bay of Fundy, into which it falls in 45° 5' N. It forms throughout its entire course the line of separation between the state of Maine in the N American federacy, and the British prov. of New Brunswick. Its head-streams are the Cheputnecticook and the Schoodie; the former is the E, the latter the W branch. These two streams unite above what is called the upper fall of the Sainte C., 40 m. from its mouth. The average breadth of the two streams at this place is 60 yards. The mouth of the Sainte C. is 14 m. wide, and near its centre is a little low island of the same name. The river is navigable for large ships to St. Stephen's, 17 m. above St. Andrew's. The Cheputnecticook branch descends from a series of lakes, the most northern of which receives a stream called Monument brook, from the source of which the 'due north' line was taken by the commissioners under the treaty of 1814 to settle the boundary. There can be little doubt that the Schoodie, which approaches within 15 m. of the Penobscot, and within 2 m. of one of its tributaries, forms "the most western source" of the Sainte C., whence, according to the first grant of Nova Scotia, by James I., the boundary line was to run "towards the N to the nearest ship-station, river, or source, falling into the great river of Canada."

CROIX (SAINTE), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Sarthe, cant. of Le Mans. Pop. 1,346.— Also a commune and town in the dep. of the Saôneet-Loire, cant. of Montpont, 4 m. S of Louhans, on the 1. bank of the Solnan. Pop. 1,089.—Also a commune in the dep. of Aveyron, cant. of Villeneuve. Pop. 1,393.—Also a village in the dep. and on the l. bank of the Drôme, cant. and 5 m. W of Die. Pop. 300. It has some woollen manufactures.—Also a village in the dep. of the Haute-Garonne, cant. of Fronton, 13 m. N of Toulouse. Pop. 436. It has a glass-work. -Also a village in the dep. of the Nord, cant. of Roubaix, 5 m. NE of Lille. Pop. 1,168. It has some woollen spinning-mills, and in the environs are quarries of free-stone.—Also a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, district and 8 m. W of Granson, at the foot of the Chasseron. Its chief manufactures are clocks, cutlery, and lace.

CROIX (SAINTE), a seignory of Lower Canada, in the co. and extending along the NE side of the s. of Lotbinniere, and bounded on the N by the St. Lawrence. It possesses a fine soil, and, with the exception of the swampy districts, abounds with excellent timber. A river of the same name, an affluent of the Gouffre, has its source in this s.—Also a river of the North West Territory, U.S., which takes its rise in the Upper Sainte-Croix lake, runs SSW, passes through Lower Sainte-Croix lake, and after a total course of 165 m., navigable in its entire extent, unites with the Mississippi. A communication with Bois Brulee river connects the Sainte Croix with Lake Superior. CRO

CROIX (SAINTE), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Ariege, arrond. of Saint- Girons. The cant. comprises 11 com. Pop. in 1831, 8,124; in 1841, 8,559. The town, called also Sainte- Croix-de-Volvestre, is 12 m. N of Saint-Girons, on the r. bank of the Volgi. Pop. in 1846, 1,886. It possesses manufactories of varieties of woollen fabrics and of pottery, and has 6 annual fairs.
CROIX (SAINTE). See CRUZ (SANTA).
CROIX-SUR-BUCHY (SAINTE), a village of France, in the dep. of the Seine-Inferieure, cant. of Buchy, 16 m. NE of Rouen. Pop. 524. It has an annual cattle fair.

CROIX-FONSOMME (SAINTE), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Aisne, cant. of Bohain, 10 m. from Saint-Quentin. Pop. 369. It has manufactures of shawls. Hops form the chief article of local culture.
CROIX-DANS-LA-HAYNE (SAINTE), a town of France, in the dep. of the Manche, cant. of Beaumont, 8 m. W of Cherbourg.

CROIX - AUX - MINES (SAINTE), or HEILIGKREUTZ-IM-LEBERTHAL, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haut-Rhin, cant. of Sainte-Marieaux-Mines, 16 m. NW of Colmar. Pop. in 1841, 3,625. Cotton and wool-spinning, the manufacture of cotton fabrics and of oil, and the distillation of brandy, form the chief branches of local industry.
CROIX-DU-MONT (SAINTE), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Gironde, cant. of Cadillac, 4 m. from Bordeaux. Pop. 1,126. It is noted for its wine.
CROIX-DE-MONTFERRAND (SAINTE), a village of France, in the dep. of the Dordogne, 20 m. SE of Bergerac. Pop. 544.
CROIX - EN - PLAINE (SAINTE), or HEILIGKREUTZ-AUF-DER-EBENE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Haut-Rhin, cant. and 4 m. S of Colmar. Pop. in 1841, 1,609.
CROIXILLE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, cant. of Chailland. Pop. 1,047.

CROKER ISLAND, an island of the South Pacific, near the N coast of Australia, at the entrance of Mountnorris bay, and separated by Bowen strait from the E extremity of Coburg peninsula. It is 214 m. in length from N to S, and from 2 to 5 m. broad. It is terminated on the N by a cape of the same name, in S lat. 10° 58′ 30″, E long. 132° 34′ 10″.—Also an island in S lat. 17° 26', E long. 143° 23'.
CROKER RANGE, a mountain ridge of Southeastern Australia, extending between the Lachlan and the Macquarie.
CROKER (Mount), a mountain in Arctic America, between the parallels of 73° 53' and 74° N, and in W long. 90°.

CROKER'S RIVER, a river of South-eastern Australia, which unites with Apsley river, in S lat. 31° 8'.—Also a river of Arctic America, which has its embouchure between Point Tinney and Point Clifton, in 119° 5' W long.
CROLLES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Isère, cant. of Le Touvet, 12 m. NE of Grenoble. Pop. 1,511. It produces good red wine.
CROMA (LA), or CHROMA, a small island in the Adriatic, opposite the town of Ragusa. Richard Cœur-de-Lion landed here on his return from the Holy Land.
CROMAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Vienne, cant. of Saint Sulpice-les-Feuilles. Pop. 1,037.
CROMAR, a division of the district of Marr, comprising the parishes of Coul, Tarland, Migvy, Logie- Coldstone, and part of Tulloch.

CROMARTY, a very small county of Scotland, washed on three sides by the friths of C. and of Moray, and bounded on the W by the co. of Ross. Its extreme length is about 16 m.; and it is, on an average, about 63 m. or 7 m. in breadth; but is intersected by a large tract of common, called the Mulbuie or Mulbuy, which belongs to Ross-shire, and by the district of Ferintosh which is in the co. of Nairn. The whole peninsula has the name of Ardmeanach, or 'the Black isle;' and the C. part is called 'the old shire of C.' This district was in very early times a sheriffdom, hereditary in the family of Urquhart of Throat. C. It formed a tract whose greatest length was 10 m., and average breadth 13 m. The area, therefore, was only 171 sq. m. To the south of this district, and in the middle of the peninsula, lies the extensive common named the Mulbuie. The rest of this co. consists of nine detached portions scattered up and down in various parts of Ross-shire, containing in all about 344 sq. m., or 220,586 acres. It has been found necessary, in all bills relating to roads, bridges, &c., to include the whole of these annexations in Ross-shire. A long ridge of hills extends through the middle of the co., having a fine declivity on either side towards the shores of the two friths. The higher grounds are mostly covered with heath; but towards the shores the soil is light and early. Freestone, granite, and reddish-coloured porphyry, are almost the only minerals. Fisheries are very successfully carried on, and pearls of considerable value are sometimes found in the frith of C., where the river Conal falls into that bay. C. contains only one town-from which the county takes its name-which was formerly a royal burgh, and 5 parishes. The language spoken is generally Gaelic; but many speak that broad Scottish patois commonly called the Buchan or Aberdeenshire dialect. The district is comprehended in the sheriffdom of Ross-shire; and a sheriff-substitute holds courts every alternate Friday at the town of C. It also joins with the co. of Ross in returning a member to parliament. C. gave the title of earl to a branch of the Mackenzies of Seaforth. The valued rent of Cromartyshire is £12,897 Scots; the real land rent may be estimated at £7,000 sterling. Pop. in 1801, 3,052; in 1811, 5,481. In all the more recent returns this shire is included with that of Ross: which see.

CROMARTY, a parish in the above co., about 7 m. in length, and from 1 to 4 m. in breadth, bounded by the frith of C. on the N. Pop. in 1801, 2,413; in 1831, 2,901; in 1841, 2,662.—The town of C., a neat and clean, but irregularly built place, is 19 m. NE of Inverness, and 175 m. N by W of Edinburgh. It is situated upon a low point of land which stretches out into the sea in a picturesque manner. The harbour of C. is excellent; vessels of 350 or 400 tons may lie in it in perfect security. A considerable trade in sackcloth has been long established in C. and the neighbourhood. It also exports pickled pork, hams, and dried cod-fish; but its staple trade was, until lately, the catching and curing of herrings. Pop. of the town in 1801, 1,993; in 1841, 1,938. C. unites with Wick, Dingwall, Dornoch, Kirkwall, and Tain, in returning a member to parliament.

CROMARTY FRITH, called by Buchanan the Portus salutis, one of the finest bays in Great Britain, is divided from the Moray frith by the county of C., and washes the S shore of the co. of Ross. It is about 17 m. in length, and from 3 to 5 m. in breadth. Its average depth is from 9 to 12 fath. The entrance is between two twin promontories or headlands called the Sutors of C., two bluff wooded hills, which are about 13 m. distant from each other; above which the frith expands into a beautiful bay of about 6 m. in length and in breadth. There is fine anchoring-ground, after passing the Sutors, for several miles up the bay, with deep water on both sides almost close to the shore. A ferry-boat is established across the bay from the Ross to the C. side. Pop. 732.

CROMBIE POINT, a village in the p. and 3 m. E of Torryburn, Fifeshire, 6½ m. NW of North Queensferry. Pop. 54. The Newhaven and Stirling lonika. CRO steamers touch here. Crombie was formerly a distinct parish.
CROMDALE, a large parish partly in Invernessshire, partly in Elginshire, intersected by the Spey river. Pop. in 1801, 2,187; in 1841, 3,561.

CROMER, a parish of Norfolk, 13 m. SW of Wells and 21 m. N of Norwich. Area 800 acres. Pop. 1,240. The town is situated on a high cliff, commanding a beautiful view of Cromer bay, called by seamen, from its dangerous navigation, the Devil's

CROMFORD, or CRUMBEFORD, a chapelry and village in the p. of Wirksworth, Derbyshire, 2 m. from Matlock, and 15 m. NNW of Derby, pleasantly situated on the Derwent. Pop. in 1801, 1,115; in 1831, 1,291; in 1841, 1,407. This flourishing township rose into importance, and was rendered eminent and remarkable, by Sir Richard Arkwright, the regenerator or rather originator of the cotton manufacture, who purchased the manor in 1789, having previously established here, in 1771, the first extensive cotton-mills that ever were erected in England or worked by water-power. They are supplied by a never-failing stream of water, drained from the mines on C. moor, which not only never freezes itself, but also prevents the adjoining canal from being frozen throughout the winter. The mills, 2 in number, and houses for the workmen, are built of gritstone procured in the township; which, with the mills themselves, have since continued principally to belong to the family of Sir Richard, who have another mill at Masson, a little higher up the Derwent. In 1838, these mills employed 612 hands, 64 of whom were children, generally paupers, who had previously been taught for a certain period at a free school built and supported by the proprietors of the mills. The lead mines of Wirksworth, the manufacture of red lead, the grinding and preparing of calaminaris, the wharfs, the canal and the railroads, together with extensive smelting mills, a hat manufactory, and worsted mills, at Lea, in the immediate vicinity, not only give employment to the pop. of the township, but render it -especially the town of C.-of commercial importance. In 1790, Sir Richard Arkwright obtained a grant of a market, which is now held every Saturday. The Rock-house, the seat of the Arkwrights, is built on a high limestone rock, overlooking a beautiful part of the Derwent vale.—The C. canal joins the Erwash canal at Langly bridge; and the Great North Midland railway crosses the C. canal between Crich and Pentrick. The High Peak or C. and Peak Forest railway begins at this canal 1 m. to the SE of C., and terminates at the Peak Forest canal at Whalley-bridge. The length of this railway is about 34 m., and in its course it passes over some high land; its greatest elevation being 990 ft. above the level of the C. canal. This ascent is accomplished by means of several inclined planes, up which the waggons are drawn by stationary steam-engines. The summit level is maintained for 12 m., and in its course the railway passes through a hill by means of a tunnel 638 yards long. Contrary to the now almost universal practice, cast-iron rails have been adopted instead of malleable iron. They are cast into lengths of 4 ft. each, which weigh 84 lbs., or 63 lbs. per yard.—The Manchester, Buxton, and Matlock railway unites C. with the Midland Junction railway at Ambergate station, from which it is 5 m. distant, and with Matlock, Rowsley, and Bakewell.
CROMHALL-ABBOT'S, a parish in Gloucestertershire, 21 m. WNW of Wicker. Area 2,810 acres.
CROMIDI, a village of Turkey, in Macedonia, on the E side of the gulf of Salonika, 45 m. SSE of Sa-
CROMPTON, a township in the p. of Oldhamcum-Prestwich, Lancashire, 4 m. SSE of Rochdale, Area 2,290 acres. Pop. 6,729. Cottons are extensively manufactured here.
CROMWELL, a parish in Nottinghamshire, 5 m. N of Newark. Area 1,170 acres. Pop. 203.-Also a township in Huntingdon co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U.S. Pop. 1,140.
CRONACH. See KRONACH.
CRONAT, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Bourbon-Lancy, near the r. bank of the Loire. Pop. 1,466.

CRONBANE, a metalliferous mountain, co. Wicklow, on the l. bank of the Ovoca, 3 m. S by E of Rathdrum. Other mountains and hills in the immediate vicinity are metalliferous,-particularly Connery and Tigrony, on the same bank of the river as C., and Ballymurtagh, Ballygahan, Kilcashel, and Knockanade on the opposite bank; but C. is regarded as the most productive, and, together with Ballymurtagh, gives name to the whole mineral field. The most valuable mineral stratum yet discovered is situated in the W side of C., and consists principally, in the upper part, of black copper ore, and, in the under and very deep part, of copper and iron pyrites. C. produced, during the first 12 years, about 7,530 tons of ore; but, in consequence of the great and permanent depreciation which occurred in 1808, it has since been worked to the extent of only a few tons per ann. By an ingenious process for procuring a precipitate from the superfluent waters of the mine, upwards of £12,000 worth of copper was obtained at the expense of only £2,620 for iron. Sulphur has also been manufactured by extract from the copper pyrites.
CRONBERG. See Elsinore.
CRONDALL, a parish in Hants, 3 m. NW of Farnham. Area 9,540 acres. Pop. 2,199.
CRONCELS, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Aube, com. of Troyes. Pop. 1,857.
CRONE. See KRONE.
CRONIERE (LA), an island of France, off the coast of Vendée, opposite the island of Noirmoutiers. Pop. 250. At low tide one may pass on horseback from the mainland to this island.

CRONSTADT, or Bunzenland, a district in that part of the SE portion of Transylvania, called 'the Land of the Saxons;' bounded N and E by Upper Weissemburg and Haromszek; on the S and SW by Wallachia; and on the W by Fogaras. It is 36 m. long, and 21 m. broad; and has a surface of about 690 sq. m. It is elevated and very mountainous. On the S side, towards Wallachia, it is enclosed by the Carpathians, across which are three grand passes, the Türtzburg, the Tömös or Temesch, and the Alte- Shantze or Altschanz. The principal river is the Aluta, which receives the waters of the Tatsang, Burcze, Vieden, and others. The climate is generally temperate, but variable; and during the prevalence of the E wind, or krivitz as it is here called, the cold is often severe. The soil produces grain, flax, hemp, fruits, and wood; but the variableness of the temp. prevents the successful culture of the vine. The pop. is chiefly employed in agriculture. The mineral productions are gold, silver, lead, and porcelain earth. The mountains are richly wooded; and on the banks of the rivers, large herds of cattle are pastured. Game and fish are pretty abundant.—The pop., amounting to 97,721 in 1837, is composed of Saxons, Magyars, Wallachians, Greeks, and Armenians.—The dist. is divided into 3 fiscal, and 4 seignorial circles; and contains 4 market-towns and 25 villages. The principal towns, besides the cap., are Marienburg, Zeiden, Rosenau, and Tortzburg. CRONSTADT, OF KRUHNEN, [HUNGARIAN, Brassó,] TO COMPANY AND THE SECOND OF THE SECOND AND AND A SECOND ASSESSMENT OF THE SECOND ASSESSMENT OF THE SECOND ASSESSMENT OF THE SECOND ASSESSMENT OF THE SECOND ASSESSMENT OF THE SECOND ASSESSMENT OF THE SECOND ASSESSMENT OF T CRO able town of Transylvania, is situated in N lat. 45° 36½', E long. 25° 33' 32", at an alt. of 1,922 ft. above sea-level, at the foot of a hill, on the summit of which stands the citadel, a modern fortress of some strength. The mountains come close down on the little valley in which the town stands. It is surrounded by walls; and has 3 suburbs, -the Altstadt, Blumenau, and Bulgarei or Upper town. A rapid stream rushes in various channels through the streets. The Protestant high church, and the Lutheran church of St. Bartholomew, are fine buildings. The town possesses a Lutheran gymnasium, a Catholic normal school, several elementary schools, two hospitals, and a house-ofindustry. The manufactures of C. consist of linens, cottons, coarse woollens, stockings, leather, wooden bottles, and light waggons. There is a paper-mill, and an extensive printing-office. C. was the first place in Transylvania where a printing-press was established. C. is an important commercial town, and has a considerable transit trade, chiefly between Vienna and the Principalities, in cattle, wine, corn, salt, and manufactured goods. Its transactions are estimated to amount to at least £1,000,000 per ann. The Kauphaus, or great mart of C., is the place of rendezvous for merchants of all nations, Saxons, Greeks, Turks, Jews, Hungarians, Bulgarians, Wallachians, and Gypsies. The pop. in 1789 was 18,118; it is supposed now to amount to 36,000, which is by far the greatest of any town in Transylvania.

CRONSTADT, a strongly fortified town and seaport of Russia, in the gov. of St. Petersburg, seated on the SE point of the low island of Kotlin or Retusari, which is about 7 m. long and 1 m. broad, in the gulf of C., formed by the estuary of the Neva in the gulf of Finland, in N lat. 59° 59′ 26″, E long. 29° 49′ 30″, 31 m. W of St. Petersburg. On the NW extremity of the island is a lighthouse. The town is regularly built; the streets are straight and well-paved; most of the houses are only one story high, built of wood and stuccoed; the government-offices and several handsome edifices, however, are of stone. Among the principal buildings at C. are the imperial marine hospital, which is on a very extensive scale, the civil hospital, the barracks, custom-house, exchange, school for pilots, and the cathedral. C. has three harbours. The outer one, which is surrounded by a mole, and stretches out into the sea, at low water is so shallow that large vessels are obliged to anchor in the middle harbour, which is chiefly used for the fitting-out and repairing of vessels, and contains the slips, a powder-magazine, and several of the founderies and minor manufactories connected with shipbuilding. Parallel with this is the W or innermost harbour, having accommodation for 600 merchantships. The other two harbours are exclusively confined to the use of the imperial navy. There is a good roadstead outside the port, which is defended by the citadel. There are no building slips at C. Vessels are repaired in a granite basin communicating with the military canal of Peter the Great, 31 furlongs in length and about 30 yards wide, running between the Middle and Merchants' harbour. The Catherine canal communicates with the Merchants' port. The freezing of the Neva prevents vessels entering or departing from the bay of C. throughout the period from the end of November to the end of April; and as the inhabitants of C. derive their chief support from the shipping, the town in the winter season presents a very dreary aspect. In summer the pop. may amount to about 40,000; but in winter is greatly diminished. The city of C. has three gates, and is divided into two parts,—the commandant, and admiralty quarters, - which are subdivided into 4 districts. The town and port were founded in 1702 the cap. of the above district, and the most consider- | by Peter the Great; who, it is said, employed upwards of 300,000 men on their formation. The fortifications were completed by his daughter Elizabeth in 1721. At the entrance of the harbour lies the castle of Kronslot or Cronschloth. This fortress and the mole bristle with guns, and the harbour itself is approachable only by one channel which is fortified with a double line of guns. These works constitute C. "the Malta of the Baltic." Besides being of importance as the great naval station for the Russian fleet, C. is the harbour and grand arsenal of St. Petersburg. The channel is marked by stakes the whole way, and vessels built at Petersburg, are placed on a camel or kind of raft, by which their draught of water is lessened one-half, and then floated down the Neva, and over its bar on which there is often only 7 ft. water. All vessels proceeding to the cap. are searched at C. and their cargoes sealed. If too large for the shallow waters of the Upper Neva, they are unloaded here, and their cargoes transported in smaller craft to St. Petersburg. The exports of C. are iron, hemp, flax, tar, oil, and linseed. The navigation returns for the port of C. show that at the commencement of 1849 there were in port 37 sailing-vessels which had 728. wintered there, which, with the vessels and steamers arrived during the year, together with 4 new ships, give a total of 1,611 vessels, of which 940 were English. Besides these there were 438 coasting sailing-vessels and 56 coasting-steamers. Of the above 1,611, 45 were Russian vessels, which had sailed as coasters. The sailings amounted to 1,550, of which 860 were English vessels. The season commenced on the 15th of May, and ended on the 26th of Nov. The number of vessels which entered the port in <math>1829 \text{ was } 1,510 = 267,054 \text{ tons}</math>; of which 684 were in ballast; 352 were British ships with cargoes, and 421 were British ships in ballast. In 1815, 1,230 vessels sailed from C., of which 641 were English.
CRONTON, a township in the p. of Prescot, Lancashire, 31 m. SSE of Prescot. Area 1,000 acres. Pop. 402.
CROOK, a chapelry in the p. of Kendal in Westmoreland, 42 m. W by N of Kendal. Pop. 257.- Also a p. in co. Waterford, 5 m. E by S of Waterford. Area 1,935 acres. Pop. 814.
CROOK AND BILLYROW, a township in the p. of Brancepeth, Durham, 51 m. NNW of Bishop- Auckland. Pop. 538.
CROOK-OF-DEVON, a village of Perthshire, in the p. of Fossaway, 6 m. W of Kinross, on the river Devon. Pop. 61.
CROOKDALE, a township in the p. of Bromfield in Cumberland. Pop. 191.

CROOKED-CREEK, a village in Tioga co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U.S., 153 m. N of Harris- 201. burg.—Also a v. in Stokes co. in N Carolina, 129 m. WNW of Raleigh.—Also a township in Carroll co., in Arkansas. Pop. 664.—Also a v. in Steuben co., in Iowa.—Also a v. in Clinton co., in Illinois.—Also a river in Cumberland co., in the state of Georgia, flowing E to Cumberland Sound.
